Step 1 — Structural sanity checks

  • Shape of RNA matrix
  • Shape of phenotype
  • Shape of survival
  • Confirm orientation (genes × samples vs samples × genes)

Step 2 — ID alignment

  • Extract patient IDs from RNA sample IDs
  • Count intersection size
  • Print number retained after merge
  • Do not proceed until this is airtight.

Step 3 — Survival inspection

  • Distribution of survival time,
  • Event rate (how many deaths?)
  • How many ≥ 5 years?
  • If event rate is tiny, modeling will be unstable.

Step 4 — RNA basics

  • Gene count
  • Remove low-expression genes
  • Check for obvious outlier samples
In [1]:
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import numpy as np
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
In [2]:
# Load data

RAW_DIR = "../data/raw"

rna_path = f"{RAW_DIR}/TCGA-BRCA.star_tpm.tsv.gz"
clin_path = f"{RAW_DIR}/TCGA-BRCA.clinical.tsv.gz"
surv_path = f"{RAW_DIR}/TCGA-BRCA.survival.tsv.gz"

# Load
rna_raw_df = pd.read_csv(rna_path, sep="\t")
clin_raw_df = pd.read_csv(clin_path, sep="\t")
surv_raw_df = pd.read_csv(surv_path, sep="\t")

EDA¶

Structural sanity checks¶

In [3]:
# Check

# Shapes
print("RNA shape:", rna_raw_df.shape)
print("Clinical shape:", clin_raw_df.shape)
print("Survival shape:", surv_raw_df.shape)

# Peek at columns / headers
print("\nRNA head, tail:")
display(rna_raw_df.head())
display(rna_raw_df.tail())

print("\nClinical head, tail:")
display(clin_raw_df.head())
display(clin_raw_df.tail())

print("\nSurvival head, tail:")
display(surv_raw_df.head())
display(surv_raw_df.tail())

ID alignment¶

Check sample ID intersections between the 3 datasets¶

In [8]:
# Extract ID sets
clin_ids = set(clin_raw_df["sample"])
surv_ids = set(surv_raw_df["sample"])

# RNA sample IDs are column names (exclude first gene column if needed)
rna_cols = rna_raw_df.columns.tolist()

# If first column is gene identifier, drop it
if not rna_cols[0].startswith("TCGA-"):
    rna_ids = set(rna_cols[1:])
else:
    rna_ids = set(rna_cols)

print("Clinical N:", len(clin_ids))
print("Survival N:", len(surv_ids))
print("RNA N:", len(rna_ids))

print("\nClinical ∩ Survival:", len(clin_ids & surv_ids))
print("Clinical ∩ RNA:", len(clin_ids & rna_ids))
print("Survival ∩ RNA:", len(surv_ids & rna_ids))
print("All three intersect:", len(clin_ids & surv_ids & rna_ids))

Clinical N: 1255
Survival N: 1232
RNA N: 1226

Clinical ∩ Survival: 1232
Clinical ∩ RNA: 1226
Survival ∩ RNA: 1203
All three intersect: 1203

In [13]:
# Filter sample IDs to 1 per patient ID

# In TCGA:
# 01A = primary tumor
# 11A/11B = normal tissue
# 06A = metastatic tumor
# So filter to primary tumor only:
surv_df = surv_raw_df[
    surv_raw_df["sample"].str.split("-").str[-1].str.startswith("01")
].copy()

surv_df["suffix"] = surv_df["sample"].str.split("-").str[-1]

# 01A, 01B, 01C are primary tumor vials
# Filter to one per patient
surv_df = (
    surv_df.sort_values("suffix")   # 01A < 01B < 01C
           .drop_duplicates(subset="_PATIENT", keep="first")
)

# Check
display(surv_df.head())
display(surv_df.tail())
print(surv_df["_PATIENT"].value_counts().max())
surv_df["_PATIENT"].value_counts().describe()

Survival inspection¶

5-year survival chosen

5-Year Overall Survival Definition

  • Primary endpoint: 5-year overall survival (≥ 1825 days)
  • Event (label = 1): death within 5 years
  • Survivor (label = 0): alive with ≥ 5 years of follow-up
  • Excluded: alive with < 5 years of follow-up (censored before cutoff)

Rationale

  • Minimizes label ambiguity from censoring
  • Provides a clinically meaningful endpoint
  • Maintains adequate event rate (~33%)
  • Preserves sufficient cohort size (n ≈ 301) for stable modeling
In [14]:
# Basic survival summary

surv_df["OS.time"].describe()
Out[14]:
count    1076.000000
mean     1256.117100
std      1190.442161
min         1.000000
25%       460.250000
50%       860.000000
75%      1688.000000
max      8605.000000
Name: OS.time, dtype: float64
In [15]:
# Event rate (how many deaths)

n_total = len(surv_df)
n_events = surv_df["OS"].sum()
event_rate = n_events / n_total

print("Total patients:", n_total)
print("Number of deaths:", int(n_events))
print("Event rate:", round(event_rate, 3))

Total patients: 1076
Number of deaths: 150
Event rate: 0.139
In [16]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

plt.hist(surv_df["OS.time"], bins=50)

plt.axvline(365, linestyle="--", label="1 years")
plt.axvline(730, linestyle="--", label="2 years")
plt.axvline(1095, linestyle="--", label="3 years")
plt.axvline(1460, linestyle="--", label="4 years")
plt.axvline(1825, linestyle="--", label="5 years")

plt.legend()
plt.xlabel("Overall Survival Time (days)")
plt.ylabel("Count")
plt.title("Distribution of OS.time")
plt.show()
In [17]:
# How many ≥ 5 years (1825 days)

five_year_days = 1825

n_ge_5y = (surv_df["OS.time"] >= five_year_days).sum()
print("Patients with follow-up ≥ 5 years:", n_ge_5y)
print("Proportion:", round(n_ge_5y / n_total, 3))


# Check how many patients work for a 5 year survival cutoff

died_before_5y = ((surv_df["OS"] == 1) & (surv_df["OS.time"] < five_year_days)).sum()
alive_ge_5y = ((surv_df["OS"] == 0) & (surv_df["OS.time"] >= five_year_days)).sum()
alive_lt_5y = ((surv_df["OS"] == 0) & (surv_df["OS.time"] < five_year_days)).sum()

print("Died <5y:", died_before_5y)
print("Alive ≥5y:", alive_ge_5y)
print("Alive <5y (censored):", alive_lt_5y)

Patients with follow-up ≥ 5 years: 251
Proportion: 0.233
Died <5y: 100
Alive ≥5y: 201
Alive <5y (censored): 725

alive_ge_3y = ((surv_df["OS"] == 0) & (surv_df["OS.time"] >= three_year_days)).sum()
alive_lt_3y = ((surv_df["OS"] == 0) & (surv_df["OS.time"] < three_year_days)).sum()

In [19]:
# Filter to patients useable for 5 year survival end point

five_year_days = 1825

mask_use = (
    ((surv_df["OS"] == 1) & (surv_df["OS.time"] < five_year_days)) |
    ((surv_df["OS"] == 0) & (surv_df["OS.time"] >= five_year_days))
)

surv_df = surv_df.loc[mask_use].copy()

print("Filtered cohort size:", len(surv_df))

Filtered cohort size: 301

In [23]:
# Sample-level QC metrics
qc_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "mean_expr": rna_df.mean(axis=0),
    "median_expr": rna_df.median(axis=0),
    "sd_expr": rna_df.std(axis=0),
    "detected_genes": (rna_df > 0).sum(axis=0),
    "percent_zeros": (rna_df == 0).sum(axis=0) / rna_df.shape[0]
})

qc_df
Out[23]:

TCGA-AC-A62V-01A 1.055439 0.000000 1.955383 29648 0.511243
TCGA-AO-A0J5-01A 1.180436 0.074231 2.008713 33431 0.448879
TCGA-AO-A03N-01B 0.833770 0.000000 1.602100 26053 0.570508
TCGA-AO-A1KQ-01A 1.155044 0.073957 1.949395 32549 0.463419
... ... ... ... ... ...
TCGA-AR-A24T-01A 1.251443 0.116099 2.047498 34000 0.439499
TCGA-E2-A1IG-01A 1.183863 0.044394 2.013641 31389 0.482542
TCGA-AR-A252-01A 1.188433 0.114900 1.955514 35296 0.418134
TCGA-BH-A0EI-01A 1.252786 0.059632 2.064537 31656 0.478140
TCGA-E2-A1IO-01A 1.273338 0.096802 2.062318 33007 0.455869

300 rows × 5 columns

Sample-Level RNA-seq QC: What to Look For¶

  • mean_expr

    • Expression outliers
    • Extreme low-mean samples suggesting poor library complexity

  • median_expr

    • Expression outliers
    • Unexpected shift away from near-zero median (distribution distortion)

  • sd_expr

    • Low SD indicating compressed or low dynamic range libraries
    • High SD suggesting unstable or noisy profiles

  • detected_genes

    • Left-tail samples with substantially fewer detected genes
    • Extreme high outliers

  • percent_zeros

    • Overall cohort sparsity pattern
    • Right-tail outliers with excessive sparsity
In [24]:
# Sample-Level RNA-seq QC

metrics = qc_df.columns
n_metrics = len(metrics)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 3, figsize=(15, 8))
axes = axes.flatten()

for i, metric in enumerate(metrics):
    axes[i].hist(qc_df[metric], bins=40)
    axes[i].set_title(metric)
    axes[i].set_xlabel(metric)
    axes[i].set_ylabel("Count")

# Hide any unused subplot (since we have 5 metrics)
for j in range(i + 1, len(axes)):
    axes[j].axis("off")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
In [25]:
# Are the outliers on each graph the same sample for multiple metrics?

# Define outliers as extreme 1% tails
outlier_flags = pd.DataFrame(index=qc_df.index)

for col in qc_df.columns:
    lower = qc_df[col].quantile(0.01)
    upper = qc_df[col].quantile(0.99)
    outlier_flags[col] = (qc_df[col] < lower) | (qc_df[col] > upper)

# Count how many metrics each sample is extreme in
outlier_flags["n_outlier_metrics"] = outlier_flags.sum(axis=1)

# Samples extreme in >=2 metrics
multi_metric_outliers = outlier_flags[outlier_flags["n_outlier_metrics"] >= 2]

multi_metric_outliers.sort_values("n_outlier_metrics", ascending=False)
Out[25]:
mean_expr median_expr sd_expr detected_genes percent_zeros n_outlier_metrics
TCGA-AO-A03N-01B True False True True True 4
TCGA-E9-A245-01A True True False True True 4
TCGA-A7-A13E-01A False True False True True 3
TCGA-AO-A03T-01A True False True False False 2
TCGA-GM-A2DH-01A True False True False False 2
TCGA-BH-A1EU-01A True True False False False 2
TCGA-A2-A04P-01A True False True False False 2
TCGA-E9-A226-01A False False False True True 2
TCGA-UU-A93S-01A False False False True True 2
TCGA-AR-A251-01A False False False True True 2
In [26]:
# Filter RNA matrix

# Defined outliers as extreme 1% tails
# Remove samples extreme in >=2 metrics
samples_to_remove = outlier_flags[
    outlier_flags["n_outlier_metrics"] >= 2
].index

print(f"Removing {len(samples_to_remove)} samples:")
print(samples_to_remove.tolist())

print('Shape before removal')
display(rna_df.shape)
rna_df = rna_df.drop(columns=samples_to_remove)
print('Shape after removal')
display(rna_df.shape)

Removing 10 samples:
['TCGA-AO-A03N-01B', 'TCGA-AO-A03T-01A', 'TCGA-BH-A1EU-01A', 'TCGA-GM-A2DH-01A', 'TCGA-A7-A13E-01A', 'TCGA-A2-A04P-01A', 'TCGA-E9-A245-01A', 'TCGA-E9-A226-01A', 'TCGA-UU-A93S-01A', 'TCGA-AR-A251-01A']
Shape before removal

(60660, 300)
Shape after removal

(60660, 290)

Check expression correlation¶

  • Is overall correlation high?
    • Bulk RNA-seq samples from human tumors should show generally high pairwise correlation.
    • If most correlations are ~0.85–0.98, that’s normal.
    • If many are <0.7, something is wrong (normalization issue, mixed data types, etc.).
  • Are there strong correlation blocks?
    • Large blocks along the diagonal suggest shared structure:
      • Cancer subtype
      • Hidden batch
      • Technical processing group
In [27]:
# Rough check for duplicated samples using correlation

# Compute sample–sample Pearson correlation
corr_matrix = rna_df.corr(method="pearson")

# Find near-identical pairs (excluding self-correlation)
threshold = 0.999
duplicates = []

for i in range(len(corr_matrix.columns)):
    for j in range(i + 1, len(corr_matrix.columns)):
        if corr_matrix.iloc[i, j] > threshold:
            duplicates.append(
                (corr_matrix.columns[i], corr_matrix.columns[j],
                 corr_matrix.iloc[i, j])
            )

print("Near-duplicate pairs:")
for d in duplicates:
    print(d)

Near-duplicate pairs:
In [33]:
# Plot correlation matrix

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 8))
sns.clustermap(
    corr_matrix,
    cmap="viridis",
    figsize=(12, 12),
    xticklabels=False,
    yticklabels=False
)
plt.show()

In [35]:
# Check that one lower correlation sample
# It's a mean correlation of 0.86, the next closest is 0.88, so it's a bit of an outlier but that's still high correlation - keep it
mean_corr = corr_matrix.mean(axis=0)
mean_corr.sort_values()

Basic distribution checks for genes¶

In [36]:
# Percent zeros per gene

# What to expect:
# Bimodal distribution
# Large mass near high zero fraction (many genes rarely expressed).

gene_percent_zeros = (rna_df == 0).sum(axis=1) / rna_df.shape[1]

plt.figure(figsize=(8,6))
plt.hist(gene_percent_zeros, bins=50)
plt.xlabel("Percent zeros per gene")
plt.ylabel("Gene count")
plt.title("Gene-Level Zero Inflation")
plt.show()

# Quick summary
gene_percent_zeros.quantile([0.5, 0.75, 0.9, 0.95, 0.99])
Out[36]:
0.50    0.444828
0.75    0.927586
0.90    0.993103
0.95    1.000000
0.99    1.000000
dtype: float64
In [37]:
# Max expression per gene

# What to look for:
# A few genes far beyond the rest.
# Possible causes:
# Ribosomal genes
# Mito genes
# Annotation duplicates
# Parsing issues

# What we see:
# • Heavy right skew — expected
# • Large mass of low-max genes — most genes are weakly expressed
# • Smooth decay into a long tail
# • No obvious discontinuities or spikes

gene_max = rna_df.max(axis=1)

plt.figure(figsize=(8,6))
plt.hist(gene_max, bins=50)
plt.xlabel("Max expression per gene")
plt.ylabel("Gene count")
plt.title("Max Expression per Gene")
plt.show()

# Inspect extreme tail - in a more thorough expression analysis we would want to sync these to symbols via ensembl to make sure they make some sense
gene_max.sort_values(ascending=False).head(10)
Out[37]:
Ensembl_ID
ENSG00000211592.8     17.267480
ENSG00000159763.4     16.917957
ENSG00000124935.4     16.618061
ENSG00000198938.2     15.985453
ENSG00000210082.2     15.980333
ENSG00000163220.11    15.962754
ENSG00000198886.2     15.926417
ENSG00000198840.2     15.820402
ENSG00000198712.1     15.804347
ENSG00000198888.2     15.729046
dtype: float64

PCA¶

  • PC1 not explaining an absurd proportion of variance (>40–50%)
  • No extreme isolated outlier sample (and if present, supported by other QC signals such as low correlation or abnormal QC metrics)
  • No obvious catastrophic clustering pattern

This step is diagnostic — not for cohort pruning. We avoid data leakage by not labeling samples by survival or outcome at this stage.

If clustering were observed:

  • Step 1: Do not remove samples immediately.
  • Step 2: Investigate what is driving the structure.

Possible causes:

  1. Biological structure (likely in TCGA)

    • Different tumor subtypes
    • Different tissue of origin
    • Molecular subtypes

    Action

    • Keep samples.
    • Stratify train/test split by cancer type or subtype if appropriate.

  2. Technical structure

    • Sequencing center
    • Plate
    • Batch

    Action

    • Document structure.
    • Ensure train/test split balances technical covariates.
    • If needed, apply batch correction within training data only.

  3. Mixed biological and technical signal

    • Keep samples.
    • Address via modeling strategy rather than removal.
In [40]:
# PCA

# Transpose: samples x genes
X = rna_df.T.values

# Standardize genes (important before PCA)
scaler = StandardScaler(with_mean=True, with_std=True)
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X)

# PCA
pca = PCA(n_components=10)
X_pca = pca.fit_transform(X_scaled)

# Explained variance
plt.figure()
plt.plot(
    np.arange(1, len(pca.explained_variance_ratio_) + 1),
    np.cumsum(pca.explained_variance_ratio_) * 100
)
plt.ylim(bottom=0)
plt.xlabel("Principal Component")
plt.ylabel("Cumulative Percent Variance Explained")
plt.title("Cumulative PCA Variance")
plt.show()

# PC1 vs PC2
plt.figure()
plt.scatter(X_pca[:, 0], X_pca[:, 1])
plt.xlabel("PC1")
plt.ylabel("PC2")
plt.title("PCA: PC1 vs PC2")
plt.show()
In [ ]:
